question_title: Will Mexico officially extend or postpone the June 30, 2026 mobile line registration deadline before June 14, 2026?
summary: The probability of Mexico officially extending the June 30, 2026 mobile registration deadline before June 14 is very low (~6%). While the compliance gap is enormous (~57% unregistered) and international precedent strongly suggests eventual extension, the question's narrow timing window is the decisive constraint. The CRT has been categorically emphatic about no extension (most recently May 28), the deadline is statutory (requiring Congressional action), and Congress's current extraordinary session covers only judicial/electoral reforms with no telecom agenda. Even if the government eventually relents, extensions historically come in the last days before a deadline — not 17+ days early. The accelerating registration rate further reduces political pressure for an early announcement. The only realistic YES path would be a surprise presidential executive announcement, for which there are currently zero signals.

factors.description: CRT has categorically and repeatedly stated no extension - multiple officials (Director General Castañeda, Comisionada Presidenta Solano) since April 2026, most recently May 28, 2026

Deadline is statutory (in the law), requiring Congressional action per CRT. Congress is in extraordinary session but ONLY for judicial/electoral reforms - telecom not on agenda. Extraordinary sessions can only discuss convened topics.

Question window ends June 13, but deadline is June 30. Extensions historically come at the last minute (days before deadline, not 17+ days before). Government has no incentive to announce early and reduce registration pressure.
President Sheinbaum has publicly backed the registration program as essential for security/investigations
Registration is accelerating sharply (33% to 43% in ~1 week), showing deadline pressure is working - removing it would be counterproductive
2025 Amparo Law reform bars general injunctions against telecom regulations, narrowing judicial route to postponement
~92 million lines still unregistered (~57% of all lines) - enormous political pressure for eventual extension. Suspending 92M lines would be massively disruptive.
Universal historical pattern: every SIM registration program globally has been extended despite 'no extension' statements (Nigeria 10+, Pakistan 3-4, Mexico CFDI 4.0 extended 3 times)
Industry pressure from GSMA and América Móvil (Carlos Slim) publicly urging extension

reasoning:
**Forecast: 6% probability (YES)**

### Key Reasoning

**The critical constraint is timing, not whether an extension will ever happen.** The question asks about an official extension by June 13, 2026 — still 17 days before the actual June 30 deadline.

**Why NO is overwhelmingly likely:**
- **CRT has been categorical**: Multiple officials stated "no extension" as recently as May 28, calling it "not on the table" and noting it requires legal reform
- **Congress can't easily act**: While in extraordinary session, the agenda covers only judicial/electoral reforms — telecom is not on it, and extraordinary sessions are restricted to convened topics
- **Timing is wrong**: Even globally, SIM registration deadline extensions come at the last minute (days before the deadline), not weeks early. The government has every incentive to maintain pressure as registrations are accelerating rapidly (33% → 43% in one week)
- **The President backs the program**: Sheinbaum has publicly supported it as essential for security

**Why not 0%:**
- ~92 million unregistered lines create massive political pressure
- Every country that has attempted mandatory SIM registration has eventually extended deadlines, despite emphatic "no extension" statements
- A single presidential statement could technically trigger resolution (criterion c)
- Tail risk of surprise executive action cannot be ruled out entirely
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Mexico officially extend or postpone the June 30, 2026 mobile line registration deadline before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43754)
[Receso legislativo: aprueban mesa directiva durante pausa de 3 meses](https://www.yucatan.com.mx/merida/2026/06/01/receso-legislativo-aprueban-mesa-directiva-durante-pausa-de-3-meses.html)

[Congreso discutirá reformas judicial y electoral durante periodo extraordinario](https://www.proceso.com.mx/nacional/2026/5/22/congreso-discutira-reformas-judicial-electoral-durante-periodo-extraordinario-374517.html)

[Congreso rechaza juicio político a Rocha Moya: estas son las iniciativas para el periodo extraordinario](https://www.infobae.com/mexico/2026/05/21/congreso-rechaza-juicio-politico-a-rocha-moya-estas-son-las-iniciativas-para-el-periodo-extraordinario/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between May 31, 2026 and June 13, 2026 (inclusive, Mexico City time), the Government of Mexico officially extends, postpones, or suspends the June 30, 2026 deadline for mandatory mobile line registration such that the new effective deadline for registering existing mobile lines (or the date on which unregistered lines begin to be suspended) is later than June 30, 2026. An "official" action means any of the following: (a) a resolution, decree, agreement, or modification to the Comisión Reguladora de Telecomunicaciones (CRT) "Lineamientos para la identificación de líneas de telefonía móvil" published in the Diario Oficial de la Federación (https://www.dof.gob.mx/) that moves the registration deadline or the July 1, 2026 suspension date to a later date; (b) a legal reform published in the DOF that achieves the same effect; or (c) a formal public announcement by the CRT or the federal Executive (e.g., a press release on https://www.gob.mx/ or a statement by the CRT director or the President of Mexico) confirming such a postponement, even if the formal DOF publication follows later. The question resolves NO if no such extension, postponement, or suspension is announced or published by 23:59 Mexico City time on June 13, 2026, including if the government merely promises leniency, slower enforcement, or carrier-discretion grace periods without moving the legal deadline. Primary resolution check: search the DOF index (https://www.dof.gob.mx/) for May 31 – June 13, 2026 for any CRT or telecommunications-related publication modifying the deadline, plus reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). If the DOF is unavailable on the resolution date, credible Mexican press reports of an officially announced postponement are sufficient.
fine_print:
- "Extend or postpone" means moving the legally effective registration cutoff (currently June 30, 2026) or the suspension start date (currently July 1, 2026) to a later calendar date. Statements that registration "will continue to be available" after July 1 for already-suspended users to reactivate their lines do NOT count, because that mechanism is already part of the existing rules.
- Court-ordered individual amparos affecting only specific plaintiffs do NOT count; only a generally applicable suspension or postponement of the deadline qualifies.
- If the government repeals the registration program entirely such that the June 30, 2026 deadline ceases to apply, this resolves YES.
- Announcements made on or before June 13, 2026 (Mexico City time) by the CRT or the federal Executive count even if the formal DOF publication occurs on or after June 14, 2026.

input.anchor: Base rate for government reversing stated policy within a specific 13-day window when the actual deadline is 17+ days away: ~5%. Even in countries that eventually extended SIM registration deadlines, extensions came at the last minute (within days of deadline), not weeks before.
input.assessment:
This question has a critical timing distinction: it asks whether an extension is announced by June 13, but the actual deadline isn't until June 30. Even if Mexico follows the near-universal international pattern of eventually extending SIM registration deadlines, the timing makes a YES resolution by June 13 unlikely.

Key considerations:
1. INSTITUTIONAL BARRIERS: The deadline is statutory. The CRT says only Congress can change it. Congress is in extraordinary session but ONLY for judicial/electoral reforms - telecom is not on the agenda. Adding topics to an extraordinary session requires a new convocation.

2. TIMING MISMATCH: Extensions in similar programs globally came at the last minute - within days of the deadline. The question window (by June 13) is 17 days before the June 30 deadline. The government has every incentive to maintain pressure as long as possible, since registrations are accelerating.

3. STRONG OFFICIAL COMMITMENT: Multiple CRT officials and the President have backed the program. The "no extension" statements have been emphatic, categorical, and repeated over months.

4. COUNTERARGUMENT: The compliance gap is enormous (~92M unregistered lines). But the consequences of non-registration (suspension, not cancellation; can reactivate) are relatively soft, reducing the political pressure for an extension versus a scenario where lines would be permanently cancelled.

The most likely path to YES would be a presidential decree or executive announcement, but this would require a dramatic policy reversal with no current signals pointing in that direction. Even if the government eventually relents, the natural timing would be in the last week of June, not by June 13.
input.calibration_notes: My anchor of ~5% reflects the rarity of governments reversing firm policy positions weeks before the actual deadline. The factor analysis pushes slightly higher due to the enormous compliance gap, but the timing constraint (by June 13 vs. June 30 deadline) and institutional barriers keep the probability low. I'm not hedging toward 50% - the evidence strongly points to NO in this specific timeframe.
input.key_uncertainties: The biggest uncertainty is whether political pressure from the massive compliance gap could trigger an unexpected emergency action. A sudden political crisis or dramatic escalation (mass protests, judicial intervention) could accelerate the timeline. But there are no signals of this currently.
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent, detailed findings on current registration status, official statements, and historical precedents. web_search provided good supplementary coverage confirming no extension signals. fetch_url successfully retrieved details on Congressional extraordinary session agenda, confirming telecom is not on it. All tools performed well; no failures.
input.update_triggers: 1. Breaking news of the President ordering an extension or CRT announcement; 2. Congressional Comisión Permanente convening to add telecom to extraordinary session agenda; 3. A massive coordinated legal challenge resulting in a general judicial suspension; 4. Reports of a DOF publication modifying CRT guidelines.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was highly effective for this question, providing both current data and historical comparisons in a single call. The two parallel research queries gave complementary perspectives. The web search for Congressional session information was crucial for understanding the institutional pathway. The process worked smoothly for this type of political/regulatory question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Trace reviewed:** Full 700-line trace covering Metaculus question fetch, two research() calls, five web searches, and three fetch_url calls. All 12 tool calls succeeded (0% error rate), consistent with the agent's tool audit claim.

### Factual Claims Checked

**Factor 1 (CRT categorical no-extension statements):** Verified. The trace contains multiple sourced quotes — Castañeda's May 28 Radio Fórmula statement ("No hay discusión alguna"), Solano's April 21 confirmation, and corroborating coverage from La Jornada (Apr 1), DPL News (Apr 20), Proyecto Puente (May 29), El Heraldo de Saltillo (May 29). All grounded in research results.

**Factor 2 (Statutory deadline / Congress extraordinary session):** Verified. The agent fetched articles from Proceso and Infobae confirming the extraordinary session agenda covers only judicial and electoral reforms. The agent correctly notes that extraordinary sessions can only discuss convened topics and telecom is not on the agenda.

**Factor 3 (Timing mismatch — extensions come at last minute):** Supported by the comprehensive historical research showing CFDI 4.0 extensions came "days or weeks before each deadline" and international SIM registration extensions followed the same pattern.

**Factor 4 (President Sheinbaum backs program):** Supported in the research trace: "President Claudia Sheinbaum has publicly backed the registration, calling it essential for investigations especially in disappearance cases."

**Factor 5 (33% to 43% registration acceleration):** Verified. Research data shows 51.6M on May 27 (~33% of 158M) and 69.7M by June 1-2 (~43% of 161.7M). The numbers are correct.

**Factor 6 (2025 Amparo Law reform):** This claim originates from the Metaculus question background text rather than independent research. The trace doesn't contain independent verification of this specific reform. However, the question background is a legitimate source, and the confidence is appropriately moderate (0.8). Minor concern, not forecast-altering.

**Factor 7 (92M unregistered lines):** Verified across multiple sources (WRadio, Supercanal12, research entries).

**Factor 8 (Universal historical pattern):** Well-supported by the dedicated historical research call showing Nigeria (10+ extensions), Pakistan (3-4), India (judicial hold), Mexico's own CFDI 4.0 (3+ extensions). The trace confirms "No country has ever achieved full compliance by its original deadline."

**Factor 9 (GSMA/América Móvil urging extension):** The claim traces to the Metaculus question background. The research also independently confirms industry petition to CRT in January 2026 via El Economista article. Adequately grounded.

### Structural Checks

**Double-counting:** Factors 7 (compliance gap) and 8 (historical pattern) share the compliance-gap premise, but Factor 8 adds genuinely different international evidence. Factors 1 (CRT) and 4 (Sheinbaum) overlap somewhat as institutional commitment signals but represent distinct actors. Neither case rises to the level of problematic double-counting that would distort the forecast.

**Wrong-direction factors:** All factor signs are correct — negative logits for factors favoring NO resolution, positive for YES.

**Resolution criteria engagement:** Excellent. The agent specifically addresses the May 31–June 13 window, the DOF publication requirement, the alternative paths (presidential announcement under criterion (c)), and correctly excludes informal enforcement delays per the fine print.

**Counterargument quality:** Strong. Constructs a coherent alternative story about security theater, CFDI 4.0 precedent, resolution criterion (c) low bar, and Morena supermajority flexibility. Not a token gesture.

**Self-confidence assessment:** 0.65 confidence is appropriate given good research quality but inherent uncertainty about surprise executive action in a 13-day window.

**Probability assessment:** The 6% estimate is defensible. Decomposition: ~25% chance of eventual extension before June 30 × ~20% conditional probability of announcement before June 13 ≈ 5%, plus tail risk for surprise executive action. The factor-implied ~1.6% may be too low; the 6% represents a reasonable upward adjustment for hard-to-model political uncertainty. My independent estimate would be in the 4–8% range, well within the acceptable band of the agent's 6%.

**Anchor divergence:** Anchor 5%, final 6% — minimal divergence, well-justified.

**Tool audit consistency:** Agent claims "no failures" — ground truth confirms 0 errors across 12 calls. Consistent.

### Conclusion

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, no resolution criteria gaps, no meaningful probability disagreement. The forecast is well-researched, properly anchored, and the 6% estimate is appropriate for the narrow timing window.
